questions.explanation: Positive statement (you are) → negative tag: aren't you? "isn't" is for he/she/it, not you. "don't" belongs to main verbs, not the verb be. "are" would be a positive tag, which is wrong after a positive statement.

Negative statement (can't) → positive tag: can they? "can't" would repeat the negative — wrong rule. "do" is used for main verbs, not for the modal can. "aren't" uses the wrong auxiliary (be instead of can).
Negative statement (doesn't like) → positive tag: does he? "doesn't" would repeat the negative. "is/isn't" use the wrong auxiliary — the main verb is like, so the tag uses do/does, not be.

questions.prompt: Complete: "She called you last night, ___ she?"
questions.explanation: Positive past-simple statement (called) → negative past tag: didn't she? The main verb called uses did/didn't in the tag. "doesn't" is present tense. "wasn't" belongs to the verb be. "did" would be a positive tag — wrong after a positive statement.

questions.prompt: Complete: "You haven't seen this film, ___ you?"
questions.explanation: Negative statement (haven't seen) → positive tag: have you? "haven't" repeats the negative. "didn't" is past simple — but the statement uses the present perfect (haven't). "has" is for he/she/it, not you.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I am the last one here, ___ I?"
questions.explanation: The special tag for I am is aren't I — there is no "amn't I" in standard English. "am not" is the full form, which is not used in tags. "isn't" uses he/she/it, not I.
